docs.title: Anime FFmpeg Re-Encoder
docs.text: Automated anime encoding workflow for Google Colab

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Anime FFmpeg Re-Encoder is an automated workflow for encoding anime content on Google Colab with professional-grade quality settings. Download torrents, encode with HEVC 10-bit, and upload directly to Google Drive—all in one streamlined process.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Automated Download - aria2 multi-threaded torrent/direct downloads
  • HEVC 10-bit Encoding - Hardware (NVENC) or software (x265) encoding
  • Quality Profiles - Pre-configured presets from 360p to 1080p
  • Google Drive Integration - Automatic upload after encoding
  • GPU Acceleration - NVENC hardware encoding for speed
  • Auto Shutdown - Save Colab compute units
  • Modern Codecs - Opus audio for optimal quality/size ratio

docs.title: For Anime Fansubbers & Distributors
docs.text:
• Consistent Quality - Pre-tuned profiles optimized for anime content
  • Batch Processing - Encode entire seasons automatically
  • Free GPU Access - Leverage Colab's free T4 GPU for fast encoding
  • No Local Storage - All processing in cloud, output to Drive

docs.title: For Anime Archivists
docs.text:
• Space Efficient - HEVC 10-bit provides 30-50% size reduction vs H.264
  • Quality Preservation - 10-bit color depth maintains gradient quality
  • Automated Workflow - Set and forget, perfect for large libraries

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Configure in notebook
SELECTED_PROFILE = "Balanced 1080p"
DOWNLOAD_URL = "https://nyaa.si/download/12345.torrent"
USE_GPU = True

# Run workflow - that's it!
# Downloads → Encodes → Uploads to Drive → Auto shutdown

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Detailed configuration guide for all settings in the notebook.

docs.title: Control Panel Overview
docs.text:
All settings are centralized in Cell 2 (Control Panel):

# ===== FOLDER SETTINGS =====
TEMP_DIR = "/content/temp_downloads"
DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/Downloads"

# ===== ENCODE SETTINGS =====
USE_GPU = True
SELECTED_PROFILE = "Balanced 1080p"

# ===== DOWNLOAD SETTINGS =====
DOWNLOAD_URL = "https://example.com/file.torrent"
ARIA2_CONCURRENT = 16
ARIA2_SPLIT = 16
ARIA2_MAX_JOBS = 5
ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 0
ARIA2_BT_STOP_TIMEOUT = 300

# ===== WORKFLOW CONTROL =====
ENABLE_DOWNLOAD = True
ENABLE_ENCODE = True
ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE = True
ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN = True
SHUTDOWN_DELAY = 10

# ===== VIDEO FILE DETECTION =====
VIDEO_EXTENSIONS = ["**/*.mkv", "**/*.mp4", "**/*.webm"]

docs.title: TEMP_DIR
docs.text:
Temporary download and encoding location.

TEMP_DIR = "/content/temp_downloads"
  • Default: /content/temp_downloads
  • Location: Colab local storage (fast)
  • Persistence: Deleted when runtime disconnects
  • Capacity: ~100 GB (Free), ~200 GB (Pro)

Why /content?

Local storage is faster for encoding than Google Drive. Files are moved to Drive after encoding.

docs.title: DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER
docs.text:
Final output destination in Google Drive.

DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/Downloads"
  • Default: /content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/Downloads
  • Location: Your Google Drive
  • Persistence: Permanent
  • Capacity: Your Drive quota

Custom paths:

# Organized by series
DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/One Piece"

# Organized by season
DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/2025/Winter"

# Shared folder
DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/Shareddrives/Team/Anime"

docs.title: USE_GPU
docs.text:
Enable/disable GPU encoding.

USE_GPU = True  # NVENC GPU
USE_GPU = False  # x265 CPU
Setting Codec Speed Quality CPU Usage True NVENC 5-10x Very Good Low False x265 0.5-1.5x Excellent High

Choose GPU when:

  • Speed is priority
  • Batch processing
  • Free/Pro tier efficiency

Choose CPU when:

  • Quality is paramount
  • Archival encoding
  • Time is not critical

docs.title: SELECTED_PROFILE
docs.text:
Choose encoding quality profile.

SELECTED_PROFILE = "Balanced 1080p"

Available profiles:

  • "High Quality 1080p" - Maximum quality
  • "Balanced 1080p" - Recommended default
  • "Small 720p" - Space efficient
  • "Mobile 480p" - Mobile devices
  • "Mobile 360p" - Ultra compact

See Encoding Profiles for detailed comparison.

docs.title: DOWNLOAD_URL
docs.text:
Source file/torrent URL.

DOWNLOAD_URL = "https://nyaa.si/download/1234567.torrent"

Supported formats:

# Torrent file
DOWNLOAD_URL = "https://nyaa.si/download/1234567.torrent"

# Magnet link
DOWNLOAD_URL = "magnet:?xt=urn:btih:ABC123..."

# Direct HTTP/HTTPS
DOWNLOAD_URL = "https://example.com/video.mkv"

# FTP
DOWNLOAD_URL = "ftp://server.com/path/video.mkv"

docs.title: ARIA2_CONCURRENT
docs.text:
Maximum connections per server.

ARIA2_CONCURRENT = 16  # Default
  • Range: 1-32
  • Default: 16
  • Higher: Faster downloads (if server allows)
  • Lower: More polite to server

Recommendations:

  • Fast connection: 32
  • Normal connection: 16
  • Slow connection: 8
  • Restrictive server: 4

docs.title: ARIA2_SPLIT
docs.text:
Split download into N connections.

ARIA2_SPLIT = 16  # Default
  • Range: 1-32
  • Default: 16
  • Higher: Better for fast connections
  • Lower: Better for slow/unstable connections

docs.title: ARIA2_MAX_JOBS
docs.text:
Maximum parallel downloads.

ARIA2_MAX_JOBS = 5  # Default
  • Range: 1-10
  • Default: 5
  • Use case: Multi-file torrents (season packs)

Example with season pack:

# Download 3 episodes simultaneously
ARIA2_MAX_JOBS = 3

docs.title: ARIA2_SEED_TIME
docs.text:
Torrent seeding duration (seconds).

ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 0  # No seeding (default)

Common values:

ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 0      # No seeding
ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 600    # 10 minutes
ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 1800   # 30 minutes
ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 3600   # 1 hour

Seeding Etiquette

Consider seeding briefly to help the community. Even 10-30 minutes helps!

docs.title: ARIA2_BT_STOP_TIMEOUT
docs.text:
BitTorrent stop timeout (seconds).

ARIA2_BT_STOP_TIMEOUT = 300  # 5 minutes (default)
  • Default: 300 (5 minutes)
  • Purpose: Wait for peers/seeders
  • Increase: If download gets stuck at 99%
  • Decrease: If you want to fail faster

docs.title: ENABLE_DOWNLOAD
docs.text:
Enable/disable download step.

ENABLE_DOWNLOAD = True   # Download files
ENABLE_DOWNLOAD = False  # Skip download

Use False when:

  • Files already in temp folder
  • Manual file placement
  • Re-encoding existing files

docs.title: ENABLE_ENCODE
docs.text:
Enable/disable encoding step.

ENABLE_ENCODE = True   # Encode videos
ENABLE_ENCODE = False  # Skip encoding

Use False when:

  • Download only
  • Testing download settings
  • Moving existing encoded files

docs.title: ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE
docs.text:
Enable/disable Drive upload.

ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE = True   # Upload to Drive
ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE = False  # Keep in temp folder

Use False when:

  • Testing encodes
  • Manual file management
  • Encoding only (will download later)

docs.title: ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN
docs.text:
Enable/disable automatic runtime shutdown.

ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN = True   # Auto disconnect
ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN = False  # Stay connected

Use True when:

  • Single batch processing
  • Saving compute units
  • Unattended operation

Use False when:

  • Running multiple batches
  • Testing settings
  • Need to inspect results

docs.title: SHUTDOWN_DELAY
docs.text:
Delay before shutdown (seconds).

SHUTDOWN_DELAY = 10  # 10 seconds (default)
  • Default: 10 seconds
  • Range: 0-300 (5 minutes)
  • Purpose: Time to read logs before disconnect

docs.title: VIDEO_EXTENSIONS
docs.text:
Glob patterns for video file detection.

VIDEO_EXTENSIONS = ["**/*.mkv", "**/*.mp4", "**/*.webm"]

Default patterns:

  • **/*.mkv - Matroska files (most anime)
  • **/*.mp4 - MP4 files
  • **/*.webm - WebM files

Custom patterns:

# Only MKV files
VIDEO_EXTENSIONS = ["**/*.mkv"]

# Include AVI
VIDEO_EXTENSIONS = ["**/*.mkv", "**/*.mp4", "**/*.avi"]

# Specific folder
VIDEO_EXTENSIONS = ["SubFolder/**/*.mkv"]

docs.title: Custom Profile
docs.text:
Create your own encoding profile:

# Add to PROFILES dict
PROFILES["My Custom"] = {
    "height": 900,           # Resolution
    "crf_qp": 23,           # Quality (lower = better)
    "audio": "libopus",     # Audio codec
    "audio_bitrate": "128k" # Audio bitrate
}

# Select it
SELECTED_PROFILE = "My Custom"

See Custom Profiles for detailed guide.

docs.title: Audio Codec Options
docs.text:
Available audio codecs:

# Opus (default, recommended)
"audio": "libopus"
"audio_bitrate": "128k"

# AAC
"audio": "aac"
"audio_bitrate": "192k"

# Copy original (no re-encoding)
"audio": "copy"
# No audio_bitrate needed

docs.title: Multiple Profiles
docs.text:
Process same file with multiple profiles:

profiles_to_test = ["Balanced 1080p", "Small 720p"]

for profile_name in profiles_to_test:
    SELECTED_PROFILE = profile_name
    # Run encode workflow

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text: Common questions about the Anime FFmpeg Re-Encoder.

docs.title: What is this project?
docs.text: An automated workflow for encoding anime videos on Google Colab with professional-grade settings. Download torrents, encode with HEVC 10-bit, and upload to Google Drive automatically.

docs.title: Is it free?
docs.text: Yes! Google Colab has a free tier with GPU access. Colab Pro is optional for longer runtimes and better GPUs.

docs.title: Do I need to install anything?
docs.text: No! Everything runs in Google Colab's cloud environment. You just need a Google account.

docs.title: What makes this good for anime?
docs.text:
• HEVC 10-bit encoding preserves gradients (prevents banding)
  • Pre-tuned settings for anime content
  • Opus audio codec (better than AAC)
  • Balanced quality/size ratios
  • Fast GPU encoding

docs.title: Which profile should I use?
docs.text:
Most users: Start with Balanced 1080p

  • Archive/collect: High Quality 1080p
  • General watching: Balanced 1080p
  • Limited space: Small 720p
  • Mobile only: Mobile 480p

See Profiles Guide for detailed comparison.

docs.title: GPU or CPU encoding?
docs.text:
Use GPU (NVENC) for:

  • Fast encoding (5-10x speed)
  • Good quality
  • Most use cases

Use CPU (x265) for:

  • Best quality (archival)
  • When GPU unavailable
  • You have time to wait

docs.title: How long does encoding take?
docs.text:
For a 24-minute 1080p anime episode on T4 GPU:

  • Download: 2-10 min (varies by seeders)
  • Encode: 3-5 min
  • Upload: 1-2 min
  • Total: ~6-17 minutes

CPU encoding: 20-30 minutes

docs.title: What file size should I expect?
docs.text:
For 24-minute episode:

Profile Size Compression High Quality 1080p ~800 MB Best quality Balanced 1080p ~650 MB Recommended Small 720p ~400 MB Great ratio Mobile 480p ~250 MB Phone-friendly Mobile 360p ~150 MB Maximum save

Compared to typical H.264 8-bit (1.4 GB), saves 30-50%.

docs.title: Will quality be worse than original?
docs.text:
Depends on settings:

  • High Quality 1080p: Near-transparent, minimal loss
  • Balanced 1080p: Imperceptible for most anime
  • Small 720p: Noticeable on large screens, fine on laptops
  • Mobile: Visible quality loss, acceptable on small screens

HEVC 10-bit at same bitrate looks better than H.264 8-bit.

docs.title: What's better: HEVC or H.264?
docs.text:
HEVC (H.265) for anime:

  • 30-50% smaller files at same quality
  • 10-bit color depth (prevents banding)
  • Better for animation
  • Modern codec (widely supported now)

Stick with H.264 only if you need maximum compatibility with old devices.

docs.title: Can I preserve original quality?
docs.text:
Yes, with caveats:

  • Use "High Quality 1080p" profile
  • Use CPU encoding (x265)
  • Set CRF 18-20 for near-lossless
  • But file will still be smaller than source

True lossless is possible but defeats the purpose (files would be huge).

docs.title: What codecs are used?
docs.text:
Video:

  • GPU: hevc_nvenc (NVIDIA hardware encoder)
  • CPU: libx265 (software encoder)
  • Both output HEVC (H.265) 10-bit

Audio:

  • Opus (default, recommended)
  • AAC (optional)
  • Copy (no re-encoding)

Container: MKV (Matroska)

docs.title: What's the difference between NVENC and x265?
docs.text:
Feature NVENC (GPU) x265 (CPU) Speed 5-10x faster Slower Quality Very good Better Efficiency Good Better compression CPU usage Low High Cost Free on Colab Free on Colab

For most users, NVENC quality is sufficient.

docs.title: Why 10-bit encoding?
docs.text:
10-bit color depth:

  • Prevents banding in gradients (sky, lighting)
  • Better color accuracy
  • Actually compresses better than 8-bit
  • Standard for high-quality anime encodes

Very important for anime with lots of gradients and smooth colors.

docs.title: Does this work with non-anime?
docs.text:
Yes! The settings work great for:

  • Animation (Western cartoons, CG)
  • Screen recordings
  • Gaming videos
  • Any content with gradients

Might need adjustment for live-action (less optimal for film grain).

docs.title: Can I customize settings?
docs.text:
Yes! See:

  • Configuration Guide - All settings
  • Custom Profiles - Create your own presets

docs.title: Can I process multiple files?
docs.text:
Yes! Two ways:

  1. Download season pack: Torrent with multiple episodes
  2. Manual batch: Place files in temp folder

Workflow encodes latest file. For true batch processing, see Batch Processing Guide.

docs.title: Can I skip download and just encode?
docs.text:
Yes:

ENABLE_DOWNLOAD = False
ENABLE_ENCODE = True

# Place your files in /content/temp_downloads/
# Workflow will encode latest video

docs.title: Where do files go?
docs.text:
During processing: /content/temp_downloads/ (Colab storage)

Final output: Google Drive folder (configurable):

DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/Downloads"

docs.title: What happens to original files?
docs.text:
• Downloaded files stay in /content/temp_downloads/
  • Deleted when runtime disconnects
  • Originals are not touched (read-only)
  • Encoded files go to Drive

To cleanup:

!rm -rf /content/temp_downloads/*

docs.title: Can I seed torrents?
docs.text:
Yes:

ARIA2_SEED_TIME = 3600  # Seed for 1 hour

Default is 0 (no seeding). Set to desired seconds.

docs.title: What's auto-shutdown?
docs.text:
When enabled, runtime disconnects after successful upload:

ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN = True
SHUTDOWN_DELAY = 10  # Seconds delay

Saves Colab compute units. Disable for batch processing.

docs.title: What's the difference between Free and Pro?
docs.text:
Feature Free Pro Runtime ~12 hours ~24 hours GPU T4 (usually) V100/A100 Priority Low High RAM ~12 GB ~25 GB Price Free $10/month

Pro is faster and more reliable, but free works fine for most use.

docs.title: How long can I run?
docs.text:
Free tier:

  • ~12 hours maximum runtime
  • Disconnects after 90 minutes idle
  • Usage limits (can't run 24/7)

Pro tier:

  • ~24 hours maximum runtime
  • Less likely to disconnect
  • Higher usage limits

docs.title: What if I exceed limits?
docs.text:
Free tier:

  • "You have reached your usage limit"
  • Wait 12-24 hours for reset
  • Upgrade to Pro
  • Use CPU-only runtime (less restricted)

docs.title: Can I run this 24/7?
docs.text:
No. Colab is not for continuous operation:

  • Free tier: Definitely not
  • Pro tier: Not recommended

For 24/7 encoding, use a VPS or local machine.

docs.title: Is my data safe?
docs.text:
• Google Colab is secure
  • Files only in your temp folder and Drive
  • No one else can access your runtime
  • Standard Google security applies

docs.title: Can I use shared Drive folders?
docs.text:
Yes:

DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/Shareddrives/TeamName/Folder"

Mount shared drive same as personal Drive.

docs.title: How much storage do I need?
docs.text:
Google Drive:

  • Plan for final output size
  • Balanced 1080p: ~650 MB per episode
  • 12-episode season: ~8 GB

Colab temp storage:

  • Free: ~100 GB
  • Pro: ~200 GB
  • Enough for most batches

docs.title: What if I run out of space?
docs.text:
Drive full:

  • Free up space in Drive
  • Use smaller profile
  • Upload elsewhere (see advanced guides)

Temp full:

!rm -rf /content/temp_downloads/*

docs.title: Can I output to other cloud storage?
docs.text:
Not directly, but possible:

  • Encode normally to temp folder
  • Use rclone to upload to other services
  • See advanced guides (coming soon)

docs.title: Why is output larger than source?
docs.text:
Possible causes:

  • Source was already heavily compressed
  • Using too low CRF (better quality)
  • Source resolution lower than target

Solution:

  • Check source resolution first
  • Use same or lower resolution
  • Increase CRF if needed

docs.title: Why is output smaller than expected?
docs.text:
• Efficient compression (good!)
  • HEVC is very efficient for anime
  • 10-bit compresses better than expected
  • This is normal

docs.title: Video won't play on my device
docs.text:
HEVC 10-bit requires:

  • Modern device (2016+)
  • Hardware decoder (most devices have it)
  • Updated media player (VLC works)

If not supported:

  • Use H.264 8-bit instead (requires customization)
  • Upgrade device/player
  • Use 8-bit HEVC (requires customization)

docs.title: Subtitles missing
docs.text:
Subtitles should copy:

"-c:s", "copy"  # In encoding command

If missing:

  • Check source has subtitles
  • Some formats not supported
  • Try remuxing separately

docs.title: Where can I get help?
docs.text:
1. Check this FAQ
  2. See Troubleshooting Guide
  3. Read relevant docs section
  4. Open GitHub issue

docs.title: How do I report bugs?
docs.text:
On GitHub:

  • Include error message
  • Specify Free/Pro tier
  • GPU type
  • Profile used
  • Steps to reproduce

docs.title: Can I contribute?
docs.text:
Yes! This is open source:

  • Fork repository
  • Submit pull requests
  • Improve documentation
  • Share profiles

docs.title: Is commercial use allowed?
docs.text:
Check license, but generally:

  • Tool itself: Yes (open source)
  • Encoded content: Depends on source content license
  • Don't redistribute copyrighted anime without permission

docs.title: Why Google Colab?
docs.text:
Advantages:

  • Free GPU access
  • No local setup required
  • Good for cloud workflow
  • Integrates with Drive

Disadvantages:

  • Usage limits
  • Not 24/7
  • Internet required

docs.title: Can I run locally instead?
docs.text:
Yes! The notebook uses standard tools:

  • FFmpeg
  • aria2
  • Python

Can adapt to run on local machine or VPS.

docs.title: Does this work in other notebooks?
docs.text:
The workflow should work in:

  • Jupyter Notebook
  • JupyterLab
  • Kaggle Notebooks (with modifications)
  • Local Python environment

Colab-specific: Drive mounting, GPU allocation.

docs.title: What anime sources are legal?
docs.text:
Legal:

  • Official streams you have access to
  • Purchased Blu-rays/DVDs
  • Licensed content you own

Illegal:

  • Pirated content
  • Unauthorized torrents
  • Ripped streams from paid services

Use responsibly and legally!

More questions? Check other docs sections or ask on GitHub!

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide will help you set up the Anime FFmpeg Re-Encoder notebook in Google Colab.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin, ensure you have:

  • Google Account - For accessing Colab and Drive
  • Google Drive - With sufficient storage space
  • Web Browser - Chrome, Firefox, or Safari recommended

No Local Installation Required

Everything runs in Google Colab's cloud environment. No software installation needed on your computer!

docs.title: Step 1: Upload Notebook to Google Drive
Option A: Direct Upload
docs.text:
1. Download the notebook file: Anime_FFmpeg_Re_Encoder_Notebook.ipynb
  2. Open Google Drive
  3. Navigate to your desired folder (e.g., My Drive/Colab Notebooks/)
  4. Click New → File upload
  5. Select the downloaded .ipynb file

docs.title: Option B: Clone from GitHub
docs.text:
# In Google Colab, run:
!git clone https://github.com/syqrel/anime-encode.git

docs.title: Step 2: Open in Google Colab
docs.text:
1. In Google Drive, right-click the .ipynb file
  2. Select Open with → Google Colaboratory
  3. If Colab isn't listed, click Connect more apps and search for "Colaboratory"

First Time Setup

If this is your first time using Colab, you may need to authorize the application to access your Google Drive.

docs.title: Step 3: Select Runtime
docs.text: For optimal performance, configure your runtime:

docs.title: Free Tier Setup
docs.text:
1. Click Runtime → Change runtime type
  2. Set Hardware accelerator to GPU
  3. Click Save

docs.title: Colab Pro Setup
docs.text:
1. Click Runtime → Change runtime type
  2. Set Hardware accelerator to GPU
  3. Set GPU type to Premium (for V100/A100)
  4. Click Save

Runtime Limitations

Free tier has usage limits:

  • ~12 hours max runtime
  • Sessions may disconnect during idle
  • GPU allocation not guaranteed during peak hours

docs.title: Step 4: Mount Google Drive
docs.text:
Run the first cell to mount Google Drive:

from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
  1. Click the Play button ▶️ on the cell
  2. Click the authorization link
  3. Select your Google account
  4. Click Allow to grant permissions
  5. Copy the authorization code
  6. Paste it into Colab and press Enter

Verification

You should see: Mounted at /content/drive

docs.title: Step 5: Install Dependencies
docs.text:
The notebook automatically installs required packages:

!apt update -qq && apt install -y ffmpeg aria2 mkvtoolnix mediainfo -qq
!pip install tqdm -q

This installs:

  • FFmpeg - Video encoding engine
  • aria2 - Download manager
  • mkvtoolnix - MKV container tools
  • mediainfo - Media file analysis
  • tqdm - Progress bars

⏱️ Installation typically takes 1-2 minutes.

docs.title: Step 6: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Check if everything is installed correctly:

!ffmpeg -version | head -n 1
!aria2c --version | head -n 1
!nvidia-smi  # Check GPU availability

Expected output:

ffmpeg version 4.4.2-0ubuntu0.22.04.1
aria2 version 1.36.0

For GPU verification:

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| NVIDIA-SMI 525.85.12    Driver Version: 525.85.12    CUDA Version: 12.0     |
|-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+
| GPU  Name        Persistence-M| Bus-Id        Disp.A | Volatile Uncorr. ECC |
| Fan  Temp  Perf  Pwr:Usage/Cap|         Memory-Usage | GPU-Util  Compute M. |
|===============================+======================+======================|
|   0  Tesla T4            Off  | 00000000:00:04.0 Off |                    0 |
| N/A   45C    P8    10W /  70W |      0MiB / 15360MiB |      0%      Default |
+-------------------------------+----------------------+----------------------+

docs.title: Folder Structure Setup
docs.text:
The notebook creates these folders automatically:

/content/
├── temp_downloads/          # Temporary download location
└── drive/
    └── MyDrive/
        └── Anime/
            └── Downloads/   # Final encoded output

You can customize these paths in the configuration cell:

TEMP_DIR = "/content/temp_downloads"
DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/Downloads"

docs.title: "No GPU available" error
docs.text:
If you see this error:

  1. Go to Runtime → Change runtime type
  2. Ensure Hardware accelerator is set to GPU
  3. Click Save and wait for reconnection
  4. If problem persists, try disconnecting and reconnecting

docs.title: "Permission denied" for Drive
docs.text:
1. Disconnect and reconnect runtime
  2. Clear browser cache and cookies for colab.research.google.com
  3. Remount Drive and re-authorize

docs.title: FFmpeg not found
docs.text:
# Manually install FFmpeg
!apt update && apt install -y ffmpeg

docs.title: Slow installation
docs.text:
• This is normal on first run
  • Colab caches packages for faster subsequent runs
  • Consider upgrading to Colab Pro for faster provisioning

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Installation complete! Now you can:

  • Configure encoding settings
  • Start your first encode
  • Explore encoding profiles

Need Help?

Check our Troubleshooting Guide or FAQ

docs.title: Encoding Profiles
docs.text: Detailed guide to all available encoding profiles and their use cases.

docs.title: High Quality 1080p
docs.text:
Best for: Archival, maximum visual quality, source material preservation

SELECTED_PROFILE = "High Quality 1080p"
Parameter Value Description Resolution 1080p Full HD output CRF/QP 22 Very high quality Audio Codec Opus Modern, efficient Audio Bitrate 160k High fidelity File Size ~800 MB 24min episode Speed (GPU) 3-4 min Moderate

Characteristics:

  • Virtually transparent to source
  • Minimal compression artifacts
  • Preserves fine details and textures
  • Smooth gradients (10-bit color)
  • Best for archiving or redistribution

Use Case

Archiving Blu-ray rips or creating a permanent collection of favorite series.

docs.title: Balanced 1080p (Recommended)
docs.text:
Best for: General use, everyday watching, streaming quality

SELECTED_PROFILE = "Balanced 1080p"
Parameter Value Description Resolution 1080p Full HD output CRF/QP 24 High quality Audio Codec Opus Modern, efficient Audio Bitrate 128k Very good File Size ~650 MB 24min episode Speed (GPU) 3-5 min Fast

Characteristics:

  • Excellent quality/size balance
  • Imperceptible quality loss for most anime
  • Suitable for streaming and download
  • Fast encoding with GPU

Recommended

This is the default recommended profile for most users. Perfect balance of quality, size, and speed.

docs.title: Small 720p
docs.text:
Best for: Space saving while maintaining good quality

SELECTED_PROFILE = "Small 720p"
Parameter Value Description Resolution 720p HD output CRF/QP 26 Good quality Audio Codec Opus Modern, efficient Audio Bitrate 112k Good File Size ~400 MB 24min episode Speed (GPU) 2-3 min Very fast

Characteristics:

  • 40% smaller than 1080p
  • Still looks great on most screens
  • Ideal for laptops and tablets
  • Very fast encoding

Sweet Spot

720p offers the best quality-per-megabyte ratio. Perfect for large collections.

docs.title: Mobile 480p
docs.text:
Best for: Mobile devices, low bandwidth, very large collections

SELECTED_PROFILE = "Mobile 480p"
Parameter Value Description Resolution 480p SD+ output CRF/QP 28 Decent quality Audio Codec Opus Modern, efficient Audio Bitrate 96k Acceptable File Size ~250 MB 24min episode Speed (GPU) 1-2 min Ultra fast

Characteristics:

  • Perfect for phones and tablets
  • Low bandwidth streaming
  • Quick downloads over cellular
  • Acceptable quality on small screens

Mobile Viewing

On a 5-7 inch phone screen, 480p looks perfectly fine. Great for commutes and travel.

docs.title: Mobile 360p
docs.text:
Best for: Ultra-low bandwidth, maximum space efficiency

SELECTED_PROFILE = "Mobile 360p"
Parameter Value Description Resolution 360p Low resolution CRF/QP 30 Lower quality Audio Codec Opus Modern, efficient Audio Bitrate 96k Acceptable File Size ~150 MB 24min episode Speed (GPU) 1-2 min Ultra fast

Characteristics:

  • Smallest file size
  • Works on very slow connections
  • Suitable for small screens only
  • Maximum episodes per GB

Quality Trade-off

360p is noticeably lower quality on larger screens. Use only when size is critical.

docs.title: Visual Quality vs File Size
docs.text:
graph LR

Based on T4 GPU (Free Colab):

docs.text:

docs.title: 🏆 Best Overall: Balanced 1080p
docs.text:
Perfect for:

  • Daily anime watching
  • Streaming from Drive
  • Sharing with friends
  • Mixed device usage (PC + mobile)

docs.title: 💎 Best Quality: High Quality 1080p
docs.text:
Perfect for:

  • Archiving favorite series
  • Blu-ray rip encoding
  • Future-proofing collection
  • Large screen viewing (TV, projector)

docs.title: 💾 Best Size/Quality: Small 720p
docs.text:
Perfect for:

  • Large collections (100+ GB)
  • Laptop viewing
  • Balanced mobile/PC use
  • Limited storage

docs.title: 📱 Best Mobile: Mobile 480p
docs.text:
Perfect for:

  • Phone/tablet exclusive viewing
  • Download for offline watching
  • Cellular data streaming
  • Travel and commutes

docs.title: GPU (NVENC) Settings
docs.text:
Codec: hevc_nvenc
Preset: p7 (highest quality preset)
Tune: hq (high quality mode)
Color: 10-bit (yuv420p10le)
Rate Control: VBR (Variable Bitrate)
Lookahead: 48 frames

Benefits:

  • 5-10x faster than CPU
  • Very good quality
  • Hardware acceleration
  • Low CPU usage

docs.title: CPU (x265) Settings
docs.text:
Codec: libx265
Preset: slow
CRF: Profile-dependent
10-bit color depth
Psycho-visual optimizations:
  - aq-mode=3
  - psy-rd=2.0
  - psy-rdoq=2.0

Benefits:

  • Best possible quality
  • Better compression efficiency
  • Fine-grained control
  • No GPU required

docs.title: Audio Encoding
docs.text:
All profiles use Opus codec:

Codec: libopus
Channels: Stereo (2.0)
VBR: Enabled
Bitrate: Profile-dependent (96k-160k)

Why Opus?

  • Better quality than AAC at same bitrate
  • Modern codec (2012)
  • Excellent for voice and music
  • Widely supported

docs.title: Color Processing
docs.text:
All profiles maintain:

Color Primaries: BT.709
Transfer: BT.709
Colorspace: BT.709
Bit Depth: 10-bit

Benefits:

  • Prevents banding in gradients
  • Preserves color accuracy
  • Standard for HD content
  • Better than 8-bit

docs.title: Customizing Profiles
docs.text:
Want to modify a profile? See Custom Profiles Guide.

Example:

# Create a custom variant
PROFILES["My Custom"] = {
    "height": 900,  # Between 720p and 1080p
    "crf_qp": 23,
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "128k"
}

SELECTED_PROFILE = "My Custom"

Still Unsure?

Check our FAQ or start with Balanced 1080p - you can't go wrong!

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: Get your first anime episode encoded in under 10 minutes!

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Completed Installation
  • Google Colab notebook opened
  • Google Drive mounted

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Settings (2 minutes)
docs.text:
Find the Control Panel cell and modify these key settings:

# ===== DOWNLOAD SETTINGS =====
DOWNLOAD_URL = "https://nyaa.si/download/1234567.torrent"  # Your torrent/magnet/direct link

# ===== ENCODE SETTINGS =====
SELECTED_PROFILE = "Balanced 1080p"  # Choose profile
USE_GPU = True  # Keep True for speed

# ===== FOLDER SETTINGS =====
DRIVE_OUTPUT_FOLDER = "/content/drive/MyDrive/Anime/Downloads"  # Change if needed

Finding Download Links

Popular anime torrent sources:

  • nyaa.si - Anime torrents
  • subsplease.org - Quick releases
  • Direct download links also supported

docs.title: Step 2: Run the Workflow (1 minute setup)
docs.text:
Scroll to the Main Workflow cell and click Run ▶️

The workflow will automatically:

  1. ⬇️ Download your file
  2. 🎬 Encode with selected profile
  3. ☁️ Upload to Google Drive
  4. 🔌 Shutdown (optional)

docs.title: Step 3: Monitor Progress (5-10 minutes)
docs.text:
Watch the live output:

docs.text:
Choose the right profile for your needs:

Profile Best For File Size (24min) Speed (T4 GPU) High Quality 1080p Archival, maximum quality ~800 MB 3-4 min Balanced 1080p General use, good quality/size ~650 MB 3-5 min Small 720p Space saving, still great ~400 MB 2-3 min Mobile 480p Phones, tablets ~250 MB 1-2 min Mobile 360p Low bandwidth, small screens ~150 MB 1-2 min

Change profile:

docs.text:

docs.title: Workflow Controls
docs.text:
Enable/disable specific steps:

ENABLE_DOWNLOAD = True        # Download files
ENABLE_ENCODE = True          # Encode videos
ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE = True   # Move to Drive
ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN = False  # Auto shutdown (set False for testing)

Auto Shutdown

When ENABLE_AUTO_SHUTDOWN = True, Colab will disconnect after completion. Set to False if you want to run multiple encodes.

docs.title: 1. Start Small
docs.text:
Test with a single episode before batch processing:

  • Use a short video (~5 min) for first test
  • Verify quality settings meet your needs
  • Check output file size and quality

docs.title: 2. Monitor Storage
docs.text:
Check Google Drive space before starting:

!df -h /content/drive

docs.title: 3. GPU vs CPU
docs.text:
• GPU (NVENC): Fast encoding, good quality, 5-10x faster
  • CPU (x265): Slower encoding, best quality, better for archival

Start with GPU, switch to CPU if you need maximum quality.

docs.title: 4. Download Sources
docs.text: Supported: - ✅ Torrent files (.torrent) - ✅ Magnet links (magnet:?xt=...) - ✅ Direct HTTP/HTTPS downloads - ✅ Multi-file torrents (season packs)

For a 24-minute 1080p anime episode on T4 GPU:

  • Download: 2-10 min (depends on seeders)
  • Encode: 3-5 min
  • Upload to Drive: 1-2 min
  • Total: ~6-17 minutes

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Common issues and solutions for the Anime FFmpeg Re-Encoder.

docs.title: No files downloaded
docs.text:
Symptoms: aria2 completes but no files in temp folder

Solutions:

# Check download folder
!ls -R /content/temp_downloads

# Check aria2 output
# Look for error messages in logs

# Verify URL is accessible
!curl -I "your-url-here"

# Try manual download test
!aria2c --dir=/content/temp_downloads "your-url"

docs.title: Torrent stuck at 0%
docs.text:
Symptoms: Download shows 0% for several minutes

Solutions:

# Increase timeout
ARIA2_BT_STOP_TIMEOUT = 600  # 10 minutes

# Add trackers (for old torrents)
# Check torrent health on website first

# Try different torrent source
# Some torrents have no seeders

docs.title: Download very slow
docs.text:
Symptoms: Download speed under 1 MB/s

Solutions:

# Increase connections
ARIA2_CONCURRENT = 32
ARIA2_SPLIT = 32

# Check if torrent has seeders
# Look on torrent website

# Try during off-peak hours
# Colab network can be congested

# For direct downloads, check source server speed

docs.title: "Too many open files" error
docs.text:
Symptoms: aria2 error about file descriptors

Solutions:

# Reduce concurrent connections
ARIA2_CONCURRENT = 8
ARIA2_SPLIT = 8
ARIA2_MAX_JOBS = 2

docs.title: "No video files found"
docs.text:
Symptoms: Encoding step reports no video files

Solutions:

# Check temp folder contents
!ls -R /content/temp_downloads/

# Check file extensions
VIDEO_EXTENSIONS = ["**/*.mkv", "**/*.mp4", "**/*.webm", "**/*.avi"]

# Files might be in subfolder
# The ** pattern should find them, but verify:
!find /content/temp_downloads -name "*.mkv"

# Manually specify file
latest_video = "/content/temp_downloads/subfolder/file.mkv"
encoded_path = encode_video(latest_video, profile, USE_GPU)

docs.title: GPU encoding fails
docs.text:
Symptoms: NVENC error or codec not found

Solutions:

# Check GPU availability
!nvidia-smi

# If no GPU, switch to CPU
USE_GPU = False

# Or change runtime type
# Runtime → Change runtime type → GPU → Save
# Then reconnect

docs.title: Encoding very slow
docs.text:
Symptoms: Encoding speed under 1x realtime

Solutions:

# Use GPU if not already
USE_GPU = True

# Lower resolution
SELECTED_PROFILE = "Small 720p"

# Check GPU usage
!nvidia-smi
# Should show ffmpeg using GPU

# Check if using CPU by mistake
# Look for libx265 in ffmpeg output
# Should see hevc_nvenc for GPU

docs.title: "Out of memory" error
docs.text:
Symptoms: FFmpeg crashes with OOM error

Solutions:

# Free up RAM
!sync && echo 3 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches

# Reduce resolution

# ===== BATCH WORKFLOW =====

encoded_files = []

# Step 1: Download
if ENABLE_DOWNLOAD:
    download_success = download_file(DOWNLOAD_URL)
else:
    download_success = True

# Step 2: Find all video files
if ENABLE_ENCODE and download_success:
    video_files = []

print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print(f"BATCH COMPLETE")

docs.title: Method 3: Incremental Processing
docs.text: Process files one at a time with checkpointing.

docs.title: Checkpoint System
docs.text:
import json

CHECKPOINT_FILE = "/content/drive/MyDrive/encoding_checkpoint.json"

def load_checkpoint():
    if os.path.exists(CHECKPOINT_FILE):
        with open(CHECKPOINT_FILE, 'r') as f:
            return json.load(f)
    return {"processed": [], "failed": []}

def save_checkpoint(data):
    with open(CHECKPOINT_FILE, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

# Load checkpoint
checkpoint = load_checkpoint()
processed = set(checkpoint["processed"])
failed = set(checkpoint["failed"])

# Find all videos
video_files = []
for ext in VIDEO_EXTENSIONS:
    video_files.extend(glob.glob(os.path.join(TEMP_DIR, ext), recursive=True))

# Process unprocessed files
for video_file in video_files:
    filename = Path(video_file).name

    if filename in processed:
        print(f"⏭️  Skipping (already processed): {filename}")
        continue

    if filename in failed:
        print(f"⚠️  Skipping (previously failed): {filename}")
        continue

    try:
        print(f"\n🎬 Encoding: {filename}")
        encoded_path = encode_video(video_file, profile, USE_GPU)

        if encoded_path and ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE:
            move_to_drive(encoded_path)

        processed.add(filename)
        checkpoint["processed"] = list(processed)
        save_checkpoint(checkpoint)
        print(f"✅ Completed: {filename}")

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"❌ Failed: {filename} - {e}")
        failed.add(filename)
        checkpoint["failed"] = list(failed)
        save_checkpoint(checkpoint)

print(f"\n{'='*60}")
print(f"Batch Summary:")
print(f"Processed: {len(processed)}")
print(f"Failed: {len(failed)}")
print(f"{'='*60}")

docs.title: Parallel Encoding
docs.text:
Encode multiple files simultaneously (requires multiple GPUs or CPU cores):

from multiprocessing import Pool

def encode_wrapper(args):
    video_file, profile, use_gpu = args
    return encode_video(video_file, profile, use_gpu)

# Find all videos
video_files = []
for ext in VIDEO_EXTENSIONS:
    video_files.extend(glob.glob(os.path.join(TEMP_DIR, ext), recursive=True))

# Prepare arguments
args_list = [(v, profile, USE_GPU) for v in video_files]

# Parallel encode (2 at a time)
with Pool(2) as pool:
    encoded_paths = pool.map(encode_wrapper, args_list)

# Move to Drive
for encoded_path in encoded_paths:
    if encoded_path:
        move_to_drive(encoded_path)

GPU Considerations

Colab typically has 1 GPU. Parallel GPU encoding may not work. Use for CPU encoding only.

docs.title: Priority Queue
docs.text:
Process files by priority:

import re

def get_episode_number(filename):
    """Extract episode number from filename"""
    match = re.search(r'[Ee](\d+)|[ -](\d+)[ -]', filename)
    if match:
        return int(match.group(1) or match.group(2))
    return 999

# Find and sort by episode
video_files = []
for ext in VIDEO_EXTENSIONS:
    video_files.extend(glob.glob(os.path.join(TEMP_DIR, ext), recursive=True))

# Sort by episode number
video_files.sort(key=lambda x: get_episode_number(Path(x).name))

print("Processing order:")
for i, vf in enumerate(video_files, 1):
    ep = get_episode_number(Path(vf).name)
    print(f"{i}. Episode {ep}: {Path(vf).name}")

# Process in order
for video_file in video_files:
    encoded_path = encode_video(video_file, profile, USE_GPU)
    if encoded_path and ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE:
        move_to_drive(encoded_path)

docs.title: Conditional Processing
docs.text:
Process based on file characteristics:

import subprocess
import json

def get_video_info(filepath):
    """Get video metadata"""
    cmd = [
        "ffprobe",
        "-v", "quiet",
        "-print_format", "json",
        "-show_streams",
        filepath
    ]
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    return json.loads(result.stdout)

# Find all videos
video_files = []
for ext in VIDEO_EXTENSIONS:
    video_files.extend(glob.glob(os.path.join(TEMP_DIR, ext), recursive=True))

for video_file in video_files:
    info = get_video_info(video_file)

    # Get video stream info
    video_stream = next((s for s in info['streams'] if s['codec_type'] == 'video'), None)

    if not video_stream:
        continue

    height = int(video_stream.get('height', 0))
    codec = video_stream.get('codec_name', '')

    print(f"\nFile: {Path(video_file).name}")
    print(f"  Resolution: {height}p")
    print(f"  Codec: {codec}")

    # Skip if already HEVC
    if codec == 'hevc':
        print("  ⏭️  Already HEVC, skipping")
        continue

    # Choose profile based on resolution
    if height >= 1080:
        selected_profile = "Balanced 1080p"
    elif height >= 720:
        selected_profile = "Small 720p"
    else:
        selected_profile = "Mobile 480p"

    print(f"  🎬 Using profile: {selected_profile}")

    profile = PROFILES[selected_profile]
    encoded_path = encode_video(video_file, profile, USE_GPU)

    if encoded_path and ENABLE_MOVE_TO_DRIVE:
        move_to_drive(encoded_path)

def send_email(to, subject, body):
    auth.authenticate_user()
    service = build('gmail', 'v1')

    message = MIMEText(body)
    message['to'] = to
    message['subject'] = subject

    raw = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(message.as_bytes()).decode()
    service.users().messages().send(
        userId='me',
        body={'raw': raw}
    ).execute()

# After batch completes
send_email(
    to="your-email@gmail.com",
    subject="Batch Encoding Complete",
    body=f"Processed {len(encoded_files)} files successfully."
)

docs.title: Verify Before Cleanup
docs.text:
def verify_encoding(source, encoded):
    """Compare durations to verify successful encode"""
    def get_duration(filepath):
        result = subprocess.run([
            "ffprobe", "-v", "quiet",
            "-show_entries", "format=duration",
            "-of", "default=noprint_wrappers=1:nokey=1",
            filepath
        ], capture_output=True, text=True)
        return float(result.stdout.strip())

    source_dur = get_duration(source)
    encoded_dur = get_duration(encoded)

    # Allow 1 second difference
    return abs(source_dur - encoded_dur) < 1

# Use in batch workflow
if verify_encoding(video_file, encoded_path):
    os.remove(video_file)
else:
    print(f"⚠️  Verification failed, keeping source")

docs.title: Custom Encoding Profiles
docs.text: Learn how to create your own custom encoding profiles tailored to your needs.

docs.title: Profile Structure
docs.text:
A profile is a Python dictionary with encoding parameters:

PROFILES["Profile Name"] = {
    "height": 1080,           # Target resolution (height in pixels)
    "crf_qp": 24,            # Quality setting (lower = better quality)
    "audio": "libopus",      # Audio codec
    "audio_bitrate": "128k"  # Audio bitrate
}

docs.title: Method 1: Modify Existing Profile
docs.text:
Start with a built-in profile and adjust:

# Copy Balanced 1080p and modify
PROFILES["My Custom 1080p"] = {
    "height": 1080,
    "crf_qp": 23,           # Slightly better quality
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "160k"  # Higher audio quality
}

SELECTED_PROFILE = "My Custom 1080p"

docs.title: Method 2: Create from Scratch
docs.text:
Define all parameters:

PROFILES["Archive Quality"] = {
    "height": 1080,
    "crf_qp": 20,           # Very high quality
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "192k"  # Excellent audio
}

SELECTED_PROFILE = "Archive Quality"

docs.title: Method 3: Resolution Variants
docs.text:
Create multiple resolution variants:

# 900p sweet spot
PROFILES["Custom 900p"] = {
    "height": 900,           # Between 720p and 1080p
    "crf_qp": 24,
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "128k"
}

# 540p for mobile
PROFILES["Custom 540p"] = {
    "height": 540,
    "crf_qp": 26,
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "112k"
}

docs.title: height
docs.text:
Target video height in pixels. Width is auto-calculated to maintain aspect ratio.

Common values:

height: 2160  # 4K (3840x2160)
height: 1440  # 2K (2560x1440)
height: 1080  # Full HD (1920x1080)
height: 900   # Sweet spot (1600x900)
height: 720   # HD (1280x720)
height: 540   # qHD (960x540)
height: 480   # SD (854x480)
height: 360   # Low (640x360)

Impact:

  • Higher = Better quality, larger file
  • Lower = Faster encoding, smaller file
  • Must be even number

docs.title: crf_qp
docs.text:
Quality setting. Lower number = better quality (but larger file).

For GPU (NVENC):

crf_qp: 18-20  # Near-lossless
crf_qp: 22     # Excellent quality
crf_qp: 24     # Very good (balanced)
crf_qp: 26     # Good (smaller files)
crf_qp: 28-30  # Acceptable (very small)

For CPU (x265):

crf_qp: 16-18  # Near-lossless
crf_qp: 20-22  # Excellent
crf_qp: 23-25  # Very good (recommended)
crf_qp: 26-28  # Good
crf_qp: 29-32  # Lower quality

Guidelines:

  • Anime benefits from lower CRF (prevent banding)
  • Start with 24, adjust up/down
  • Each +1 CRF ≈ 5-10% file size reduction
  • Diminishing returns below 20

docs.title: audio
docs.text:
Audio codec selection.

Opus (Recommended):

"audio": "libopus"
"audio_bitrate": "128k"  # Excellent for most use

Quality levels:

  • 96k - Acceptable (mobile)
  • 112k - Good
  • 128k - Very good (recommended)
  • 160k - Excellent
  • 192k - Near-transparent

AAC (Alternative):

"audio": "aac"
"audio_bitrate": "192k"  # Need higher bitrate than Opus

Use when compatibility is critical (older devices).

Copy (No Re-encoding):

"audio": "copy"
# No audio_bitrate needed

Preserves original audio. Use when source audio is already good.

docs.title: audio_bitrate
docs.text:
Audio bitrate (only for transcoded audio, not "copy").

Opus recommendations:

  • 96k - Minimum acceptable
  • 128k - Recommended default
  • 160k - High quality
  • 192k+ - Overkill for most

AAC recommendations:

  • 128k - Minimum
  • 192k - Recommended
  • 256k - High quality

docs.title: Ultra Quality Archive
docs.text:
For long-term archival of favorites:

PROFILES["Ultra Archive"] = {
    "height": 1080,
    "crf_qp": 18,           # Near-lossless
    "audio": "copy",        # Keep original audio
}

USE_GPU = False  # Use x265 CPU for best quality

docs.text:

PROFILES["Stream 1080p"] = {

    "crf_qp": 25,           # Lighter compression
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "112k"
}

USE_GPU = True  # Fast GPU encoding

    "crf_qp": 24,
    "audio": "libopus",
    "audio_bitrate": "128k"

docs.text:
Preserve multiple audio streams (requires code modification):

docs.text:
Already copies all subtitles by default:

To remove subtitles:

Add video filters (requires code modification):

# Example: Deband filter for heavily compressed sources
vf += ",gradfun=1.5"

docs.title: Two-Pass Encoding
docs.text:
For best quality (requires code modification):

# Pass 1
cmd1 = ffmpeg_base + ["-pass", "1", "-f", "null", "/dev/null"]

docs.title: Create Profile Sets
docs.text:
Organize profiles by use case:

# Quality-focused set
QUALITY_PROFILES = {
    "Archive 1080p": {"height": 1080, "crf_qp": 18, ...},
    "Archive 720p": {"height": 720, "crf_qp": 20, ...},
}

# Size-focused set
SIZE_PROFILES = {
    "Small 1080p": {"height": 1080, "crf_qp": 28, ...},
    "Small 720p": {"height": 720, "crf_qp": 30, ...},
}

# Merge into PROFILES
PROFILES.update(QUALITY_PROFILES)
PROFILES.update(SIZE_PROFILES)

docs.text:

# Or reduce resolution
"height": 720  # Was 1080

docs.title: Quality not good enough
docs.text:
# Decrease CRF (higher quality)
"crf_qp": 22  # Was 24

# Or use CPU encoding

docs.title: Encoding fails
docs.text:
# Check height is even number
"height": 720  # Good
"height": 721  # Bad (odd)

# Check CRF range
"crf_qp": 18-32  # Valid for NVENC
"crf_qp": 0-51   # Valid for x265

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start conservative: Begin with lower CRF (better quality), then increase if needed
  2. Test on samples: Use short clips before full episodes
  3. Document settings: Keep notes on what works
  4. Name clearly: Use descriptive profile names
  5. Consider source: Higher quality source allows higher CRF
  6. Balance speed/quality: GPU for speed, CPU for quality

docs.title: For Different Sources
docs.text:
Blu-ray rips (high quality source):

"height": 1080,
"crf_qp": 24  # Can use higher CRF

Web rips (medium quality):

"height": 1080,
"crf_qp": 22  # Lower CRF to preserve quality

Low quality source:

"height": 720,   # Downscale hides artifacts
"crf_qp": 24
